questions.question: Explain the basic function of a 2-to-4 line decoder. What are its inputs and outputs, and how does it work?
keyPoints: A decoder is a combinational circuit that converts binary information from n input lines to a maximum of 2^n unique output lines.
For a 2-to-4 decoder, two input lines (A1, A0) can represent four different binary codes (00, 01, 10, 11).
Only the output line corresponding to the current input binary code is activated (set to 1), while all other outputs remain 0.

questions.question: Describe how you can construct a 3-to-8 line decoder using two 2-to-4 line decoders and one additional gate. Explain the role of the enable inputs in this configuration.
keyPoints: The two 2-to-4 decoders handle the two least significant input bits (A1, A0).
The most significant bit (A2) is used as an enable signal. One decoder is enabled when A2=0, the other when A2=1, using an inverter if necessary.
This combines the four outputs from the first decoder (for A2=0) and the four outputs from the second decoder (for A2=1) to create the eight total outputs.

questions.question: Decoders are often used as fundamental building blocks. Explain how a decoder, in conjunction with OR gates, can be used to implement any arbitrary Boolean function. Use a specific example of a function F(A,B,C) to illustrate your answer.
keyPoints: A decoder generates all the minterms (or maxterms) of its input variables. An n-to-2^n decoder has an output for each minterm.
The Boolean function must first be expressed in canonical sum-of-minterms form (e.g., F = Σm(1, 3, 5, 6)).

An OR gate is then used to combine the outputs of the decoder that correspond to the minterms listed in the function (e.g., outputs for m1, m3, m5, m6 are fed into an OR gate, producing the final output).
